label: The Design Argument (Fine-Tuning)
text: The Teleological / Fine-Tuning Argument for the existence of God

children.label: P1 — The fine-tuning of the universe for life is a fact
children.text: P1 — The fundamental constants of physics (gravitational constant, cosmological constant, strong nuclear force, etc.) are precisely calibrated to permit complex life. Small deviations would produce a lifeless universe.

children.label: Cosmological constant fine-tuned to 1 in 10¹²°
children.text: The cosmological constant (energy density of the vacuum) must lie within 1 part in 10¹²° for galaxies to form. Physicist Roger Penrose: the odds against our universe arising by chance are ‘enormously greater than’ 1 in 10¹⁰⁰¹²³.

children.label: Multiple independent constants compounding improbability
children.text: Fine-tuning is not limited to one constant — dozens of independent parameters (mass ratio of proton to electron, strength of electromagnetism, etc.) must all fall in narrow life-permitting ranges simultaneously.

children.label: P2 — Fine-tuning is due to necessity, chance, or design
children.text: P2 — The fine-tuning of the universe must be explained by one of three options: physical necessity (the constants had to be this way), chance (random luck), or intentional design.

children.label: A multiverse dissolves the improbability by chance
children.text: If infinitely many universes exist with varying constants, it is statistically expected that some will permit life — we simply find ourselves in one of those (the Weak Anthropic Principle).

children.label: Multiverse itself requires explanation and is unverifiable
children.text: The multiverse hypothesis requires its own fine-tuned generator (e.g., an inflationary mechanism with specific parameters). It replaces one unexplained precision with another, and remains empirically unverifiable.

children.label: We can’t reason about observer selection in single-universe models
children.text: Critics argue fine-tuning arguments fall prey to the observer selection effect: naturally, any universe we observe must permit our existence. The apparent improbability is an artifact of our perspective.

children.label: Selection effects do not explain improbability — only survivorship
children.text: Observer selection explains why we observe a life-permitting universe if one exists, but it does not explain why a life-permitting universe exists at all. A lottery winner cannot explain away the improbability of winning by noting they had to be alive to observe it.

children.label: Physical necessity — the constants could not have been otherwise
children.text: Perhaps a Theory of Everything will show the constants are uniquely determined by deeper mathematical necessity — there was no probability space to sample from.

children.label: No current physics points to unique necessity; contingency remains
children.text: No existing or candidate physics shows the constants must take their observed values. Even string theory suggests a vast ‘landscape’ of possible values. The necessity hypothesis is speculative and unmotivated.

children.label: ∴ Design is the most plausible explanation for fine-tuning
children.text: ∴ Given that physical necessity and unguided chance are both highly implausible explanations, the fine-tuning of the universe for life is best explained by intentional design by a supremely intelligent, transcendent being.

children.label: Design inference is routine in science (SETI, archaeology)
children.text: Inferring intelligent design from specified complexity is a standard scientific move — used in SETI research, cryptography, forensics, and archaeology. The fine-tuning of physics meets the criteria for such an inference.

children.label: Who designed the designer? Design regresses infinitely
children.text: If complexity requires a designer, then a designer complex enough to fine-tune a universe requires its own designer, generating an infinite regress.

children.label: The designer need not be complex in the relevant sense
children.text: The argument requires a cause adequate to produce specified complexity — an unembodied mind is not physically complex (it has no contingent physical constants). The regress objection conflates physical and mental complexity.
